Yoshitomo Nara is a blue-chip artist whose market has seen exponential growth over the last decade, particularly in the Asian market but with strong global demand. The description identifies this as a 'monumental portrait' and a 'full-body figure,' which aligns with the major acrylic on canvas works Nara produced during this peak period of his career (circa 1996-2001). The 1996 version of 'Knife Behind Back' set a record for the artist, selling for over $25 million at Sotheby's Hong Kong in 2021. While the 2000 iteration is slightly later than the seminal 1996 original, it remains a prime example of his signature style: the juxtaposition of childlike innocence (the large head, the red dress, the 'doe-eyed' appearance) with an undercurrent of psychological menace and rebellion (the sullen expression, the hidden weapon). This duality resonates deeply with collectors, tapping into themes of alienation and the complexity of the inner self. The 'luminous pearly ground' mentioned is characteristic of Nara's technique, where he builds up layers of acrylic to create a glowing, almost ethereal background that isolates the figure. Given the artist's current market standing, the iconic nature of the subject matter, and the 'monumental' scale implied, this work would command a high eight-figure sum. Comparable sales of large-scale Nara paintings from this era (e.g., 'White Ghost', 'Cosmic Girl') consistently realize between $10 million and $15 million. parsed.internal_reasoning: The artwork described and partially shown is 'Knife Behind Back' (2000) by Yoshitomo Nara. This is one of the most significant and recognizable motifs in contemporary art. Yoshitomo Nara is a blue-chip artist whose market has seen exponential growth over the last decade, particularly in the Asian market but with strong global demand. The description identifies this as a 'monumental portrait' and a 'full-body figure,' which aligns with the major acrylic on canvas works Nara produced during this peak period of his career (circa 1996-2001). Although the image provided is a cropped detail showing only the head and torso of the girl (and notably omitting the knife mentioned in the title/description), the metadata confirms the identity of the work as the full painting. The 1996 version of 'Knife Behind Back' set a record for the artist, selling for over $25 million at Sotheby's Hong Kong in 2021. While the 2000 iteration is slightly later than the seminal 1996 original, it remains a prime example of his signature style: the juxtaposition of childlike innocence (the large head, the red dress, the 'doe-eyed' appearance) with an undercurrent of psychological menace and rebellion (the sullen expression, the hidden weapon). This duality resonates deeply with collectors, tapping into themes of alienation and the complexity of the inner self. The 'luminous pearly ground' mentioned is characteristic of Nara's technique, where he builds up layers of acrylic to create a glowing, almost ethereal background that isolates the figure. Given the artist's current market standing, the iconic nature of the subject matter, and the 'monumental' scale implied, this work would command a high eight-figure sum. Comparable sales of large-scale Nara paintings from this era (e.g., 'White Ghost', 'Cosmic Girl') consistently realize between $10 million and $15 million. The valuation reflects the premium placed on his most famous motif.
key_factors: Blue-chip status of Yoshitomo Nara in the global contemporary art market
Iconic status of the 'Knife Behind Back' motif, representing the artist's signature theme of innocent menace
Prime creation period (2000), coinciding with Nara's rise to international stardom
Monumental scale and oil/acrylic medium as described, distinguishing it from smaller works or prints
Strong auction comparables, including the record-breaking sale of the 1996 version for over $25 million
